skill.name: diffdock-molecular-docking
skill.description: Diffusion-based molecular docking to predict 3D ligand–protein binding poses (blind docking) with confidence scoring; use when you need pose prediction for drug discovery or virtual screening.
skill.summary: Predict 3D ligand-protein binding poses using diffusion-based generative modeling with confidence scoring for drug discovery workflows.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 44 static analysis findings were evaluated and determined to be false positives. The skill is a legitimate molecular docking tool (DiffDock) that uses subprocess to run PyTorch-based inference models, checks Python dependencies with __import__, and includes markdown documentation. No malicious patterns such as data exfiltration, credential theft, obfuscated code, or unauthorized network calls were detected. The static analyzer's risk score of 100/100 is a gross overestimate driven by misidentified documentation patterns (markdown code blocks flagged as Ruby backtick execution, table formatting flagged as weak cryptography).

content.user_title: Predict Molecular Docking Poses with DiffDock
content.value_statement: Predict 3D ligand-protein binding poses using diffusion-based molecular docking with confidence scoring. This skill generates ranked binding conformations and supports blind docking for drug discovery and virtual screening workflows.
actual_capabilities: Generate multiple 3D binding pose predictions for a ligand-protein pair using diffusion-based sampling
Rank predicted poses by confidence score to identify the most likely binding conformation
Perform blind docking without requiring a known binding site on the protein
Support batch docking workflows for virtual screening of multiple drug candidates
Produce structured outputs including ranked SDF pose files and confidence score reports
limitations: Does not predict binding affinity (Kd, Ki) - only structural pose confidence is provided
Protein is treated as rigid during the diffusion process with no explicit side-chain sampling
May fail for highly flexible proteins or targets that undergo large conformational changes upon binding
Standard version does not model cofactors such as heme groups or metal ions unless included in the input graph

use_cases.title: Virtual Screening of Drug Candidates
use_cases.description: Quickly evaluate multiple drug candidates by predicting their binding poses to a protein target and ranking them by confidence score.

use_cases.title: Blind Docking for Unknown Binding Sites
use_cases.description: Determine where a ligand binds when no binding pocket information is available by generating diverse pose hypotheses.

use_cases.title: Learning Protein-Ligand Interactions
use_cases.description: Practice predicting binding poses and understanding structural molecular recognition through hands-on docking experiments.

prompt_templates.title: Single Molecule Docking
prompt_templates.prompt: Dock the SMILES string CC(=O)Oc1ccccc1C(=O)O to the protein structure at ./data/protein.pdb using standard settings. Save the results to ./results and show me the confidence scores.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner user running a single docking experiment

prompt_templates.title: Docking with Confidence Analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Run molecular docking for my ligand CN1C=NC2=C1C(=O)N(C(=O)N2C)C to the protein at ./target.pdb. After docking, analyze the confidence_scores.txt and tell me which poses are most reliable.

prompt_templates.title: Batch Virtual Screening
prompt_templates.prompt: I have 20 drug candidates listed in ./ligands.txt. Dock each one to the protein at ./protein.pdb. Rank all candidates by their top confidence score and recommend the best 5.

prompt_templates.title: Custom Protocol with Fine-Tuned Parameters
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up a docking experiment with 40 inference steps and 20 samples per complex. Dock the ligand CCC1=CC=CC=C1 to ./protein.pdb. After docking, analyze the score distribution and extract only poses with confidence above 0.6 into a separate file.
prompt_templates.scenario: Expert user customizing inference for higher quality results

output_examples.input: Dock aspirin (SMILES: CC(=O)Oc1ccccc1C(=O)O) to the protein at ./protein.pdb and show me the results.
output_examples.output: Created 10 ranked pose files (rank1.sdf to rank10.sdf). The confidence scores are: rank1: 0.87, rank2: 0.72, rank3: 0.65, rank4: 0.49, rank5: 0.38, ranks 6-10: below 0.30. The top-ranked pose shows aspirin binding in the active site with hydrogen bonds to Ser530 and Tyr385.

output_examples.input: I have 20 drug candidates in ./ligands.txt. Which ones bind best to my target?
output_examples.output: Docked all 20 ligands to the target protein. Top 3 by confidence score: (1) Ligand-07 score 0.91, (2) Ligand-12 score 0.84, (3) Ligand-03 score 0.78. The top pose for Ligand-07 forms stable interactions with Asp189 and Ser195 in the binding pocket. Ligands ranked 15-20 have scores below 0.25 and are unlikely to be correct.

best_practices: Always run setup_check.py first to verify that all required dependencies (PyTorch, RDKit, PyTorch Geometric, ESM) are installed before attempting docking.
Review the confidence_scores.txt output file to filter out low-confidence poses before using results in downstream analysis or publication.
Use absolute file paths for protein PDB files and output directories to prevent path resolution errors during execution.
anti_patterns: Do not treat confidence scores as binding affinity predictions - the score measures structural pose accuracy (RMSD under 2 Angstroms), not binding strength.
Do not use extremely large protein complexes without first verifying GPU memory limits, as DiffDock requires significant GPU memory for large structures.
Do not assume the rigid protein approximation works for all targets - highly flexible proteins or those undergoing large conformational changes may produce unreliable results.

faq.question: What is the difference between the confidence score and binding affinity?
faq.answer: The confidence score predicts whether the generated pose is structurally accurate (RMSD under 2 Angstroms from the true binding pose). It does not measure binding strength (Kd or Ki). DiffDock only provides structural confidence scores.

faq.question: Can I dock multiple ligands at the same time?
faq.answer: Yes. You can run batch docking by calling the inference script multiple times for different ligands or by using the batch workflow documented in the references directory.

faq.question: What input file formats are supported?
faq.answer: Protein structures must be in PDB format. Ligands can be provided as SMILES strings or as paths to .sdf or .mol2 files.

faq.question: Does DiffDock require a GPU to run?
faq.answer: Yes, DiffDock requires a CUDA-compatible GPU with sufficient memory to load the deep learning models and process protein-ligand complexes.

faq.question: How many poses should I generate per ligand?
faq.answer: The default is 10 poses. For virtual screening, 10 to 20 poses is typical. Generating more poses increases compute time but may find better conformations.

faq.question: Can I specify a binding pocket if I already know where the ligand binds?
faq.answer: DiffDock specializes in blind docking without a known binding site. If the model supports focused sampling, providing a pocket region can improve accuracy by reducing the search space.
